content:
# Acts Chapter 11 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 238-239 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_011_page_001.jpg, ACT_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now the apostles and the brothers who were throughout Judea heard that the Gentiles also had received the word of God. **2** And when Peter went up to Jerusalem, those of the circumcision contended with him, **3** saying, "You went in to uncircumcised men and ate with them."

**4** But Peter began and explained it to them in order, saying,

**5** "I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision: a certain vessel descending like a great sheet, let down from heaven by its four corners, and it came to me. **6** Gazing into it intently, I observed and saw the four-footed creatures of the earth, and the wild beasts, and the creeping things, and the birds of heaven. **7** And I also heard a voice saying to me, 'Rise, Peter; kill and eat.'

**8** "But I said, 'By no means, Lord, for nothing common or unclean has ever entered my mouth.'

**9** "But a voice answered a second time from heaven, 'What God has cleansed, you must not call common.' **10** And this happened three times, and all was drawn up again into heaven.

**11** "And behold, at that very moment three men stood before the house in which I was, having been sent from Caesarea to me. **12** And the Spirit told me to go with them, making no distinction. And these six brothers also came with me, and we entered into the man's house. **13** And he reported to us how he had seen the angel standing in his house and saying, 'Send to Joppa and summon Simon who is called Peter, **14** who will speak words to you by which you shall be saved, you and all your household.'

**15** "And as I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell upon them, just as upon us also in the beginning. **16** And I remembered the word of the Lord, how he said, 'John indeed baptized with water, but you shall be baptized with the Holy Spirit.' **17** If therefore God gave to them the same gift as also to us who believed upon the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to be able to hinder God?"

**18** And when they heard these things, they fell silent and glorified God, saying, "Then to the Gentiles also God has granted repentance unto life."

**19** Now those who had been scattered because of the tribulation that arose over Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia and Cyprus and Antioch, speaking the word to no one except Jews only. **20** But there were some of them, men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who, when they came to Antioch, spoke also to the Hellenists, proclaiming the good news of the Lord Jesus. **21** And the hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number who believed turned to the Lord.

**22** And the report concerning them came to the ears of the church in Jerusalem, and they sent out Barnabas as far as Antioch. **23** Who, when he arrived and saw the grace of God, rejoiced and exhorted them all to remain with the Lord with resolute heart. **24** For he was a good man, and full of the Holy Spirit and of faith. And a considerable crowd was added to the Lord.

**25** And he departed to Tarsus to seek out Saul, **26** and when he had found him, he brought him to Antioch. And it came to pass that for a whole year they were gathered together with the church and taught a considerable crowd. And the disciples were first called Christians in Antioch.

**27** Now in these days prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch. **28** And one of them, named Agabus, stood up and indicated through the Spirit that a great famine was about to come upon the whole inhabited world -- which came to pass under Claudius. **29** And each of the disciples, according as anyone had the means, determined to send relief to the brothers dwelling in Judea -- **30** which also they did, sending it to the elders through the hand of Barnabas and Saul.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 11 divides into two major sections: Peter's defense of the Gentile mission before the Jerusalem church (vv. 1-18) and the narrative of the gospel's advance to Antioch, culminating in the first use of the name "Christians" (vv. 19-30). The chapter is pivotal in Luke's narrative architecture -- it resolves the Cornelius episode by securing Jerusalem's assent (vv. 1-18) and then pivots to Antioch, which will become the launching point for Paul's missionary journeys.

**Manuscript condition:** Acts 11 begins in the fourth column of page 238 (folio 119r) and continues through the first three columns of page 239 (folio 119v). The text is well-preserved and legible throughout. Standard nomina sacra are employed (KU for Lord, IHU XPU for Jesus Christ, PNS/PNA for Spirit, THU/THN for God). No significant lacunae or damage affect the readings.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong overall agreement on the chapter's structure and content. Peter's retelling of the Cornelius vision (vv. 5-17) naturally echoes Acts 10, and all three translators handle the repetition consistently with their respective approaches. The primary points of divergence concern (1) the rendering of diakrinanta in v. 12, (2) the critical textual variant Hellenistas vs. Hellenas in v. 20, (3) the vocabulary for the circumcision party and their confrontation, and (4) the rendering of oikoumene in v. 28.

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as the formal equivalence approach best serves both the legal/deliberative register of Peter's defense speech (vv. 4-17) and the historical narrative of the Antioch section (vv. 19-30). EMET's contributions are drawn upon where Greek features demand preservation (especially the "wild beasts" inclusion in v. 6 and the participial construction in v. 17). PNEUMA's dynamic clarity informs several choices but is generally set aside where theological or text-critical precision is required.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 2 -- "contended" (diekrinonto):** EMET renders "were disputing"; LOGOS renders "contended"; PNEUMA renders "challenged." The Greek diekrinonto (middle voice of diakrinō) carries the sense of contending or taking issue. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "contended" preserves the formal register and the important verbal link to diakrino in v. 12, where the Spirit tells Peter to make "no distinction" -- the same root word used by his critics.

2. **Verse 3 -- "uncircumcised men":** EMET renders literally "men having uncircumcision" (andras akrobystian echontas); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "uncircumcised men." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA: the participial phrase is a standard Greek periphrasis for "uncircumcised men," and the English adjective captures the sense without loss.

3. **Verse 6 -- Inclusion of "wild beasts" (kai ta thēria):** All three translators include "wild beasts" in the list of creatures, consistent with Sinaiticus. This is notable because Acts 10:12 in Sinaiticus omits this category. Peter's retelling adds "wild beasts" to his description of the vision's contents -- either reflecting a fuller memory or a rhetorically expanded account. SOPHIA preserves the four-category list as read in the manuscript: four-footed creatures, wild beasts, creeping things, and birds.

4. **Verse 9 -- "call common" vs. "make common":** EMET renders "you shall not make common" (koinou); LOGOS renders "you must not call common"; PNEUMA renders "you must not call impure." Consistent with the SOPHIA decision in Acts 10:15, SOPHIA renders "you must not call common" -- preserving both the declarative force of the verb and the specific term koinon.

5. **Verse 11 -- "in which I was" vs. "in which we were":** EMET reads "in which I was" (ēmēn, first person singular); LOGOS reads "in which we were." Examining the manuscript, the Sinaiticus reading is HMHN, which is the imperfect first person singular of eimi. SOPHIA follows EMET: "in which I was," as Peter is recounting his personal experience.

6. **Verse 12 -- "making no distinction" (mēden diakrinanta):** This is a theologically loaded phrase. EMET renders "doubting nothing"; LOGOS renders "making no distinction"; PNEUMA renders "without hesitation." The verb diakrinō carries both senses -- to doubt/hesitate and to distinguish/discriminate. In this context, SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "making no distinction" captures the deeper theological point. The Spirit's command to Peter is not merely about overcoming hesitation but about abolishing the Jew/Gentile distinction. The verbal echo with v. 2 (diekrinonto -- "contended") reinforces this: the circumcision party "drew distinctions," but the Spirit commanded Peter to make "no distinction."

7. **Verse 17 -- "the same gift" (tēn isēn dōrean):** EMET renders "the equal gift"; LOGOS renders "the same gift"; PNEUMA renders "the same gift." The Greek isēn means "equal" or "same." SOPHIA renders "the same gift" (2-to-1), which communicates the critical point that the Spirit given to Gentiles was identical in nature to what the apostles received at Pentecost.

8. **Verse 18 -- "repentance unto life" (tēn metanoian eis zōēn):** EMET and LOGOS both preserve the phrase "repentance unto life." PNEUMA renders "the chance to turn to him and live." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS: "repentance unto life" is a precise theological formula -- metanoia (repentance) leading to zōē (life) -- and PNEUMA's paraphrase, while vivid, loses the technical formulation.

9. **Verse 20 -- Hellenistas vs. Hellenas (critical variant):** This is the most significant textual issue in the chapter. EMET reads "Greeks" but notes Sinaiticus has Hellenistas; LOGOS reads "Greeks" and notes Hellenas; PNEUMA reads "Greeks" but notes Hellenistas. The Sinaiticus manuscript reads ELLHNISTAS (Hellenistas). This is a notoriously difficult textual problem. Hellenistas ("Hellenists") normally refers to Greek-speaking Jews (cf. Acts 6:1, 9:29), while Hellenas ("Greeks") means ethnic Gentiles. The context (contrasting with preaching "to no one except Jews" in v. 19) demands that a new, non-Jewish audience is being reached. Two possibilities exist: (a) the original reading was Hellenas and a scribe confused it with the similar Hellenistas, or (b) the original was Hellenistas but Luke used it here in a broader sense to mean "Greek-speakers" including Gentiles. SOPHIA preserves the Sinaiticus reading "Hellenists" in the text, as our governing principle is fidelity to the manuscript. A footnote-level explanation is provided in the Council Notes and Disagreement Log. The narrative context makes the intended meaning clear regardless of the precise term.

10. **Verse 22 -- "sent out" (exapesteilan):** EMET renders "sent out"; LOGOS renders "sent out"; PNEUMA renders "sent." The compound verb exapostellō (with the prefix ex-) carries the sense of formal commissioning or dispatching. SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1): "sent out" preserves the compound force.

11. **Verse 23 -- "resolute heart" (prothesis tēs kardias):** EMET renders "purpose of heart"; LOGOS renders "resolute heart"; PNEUMA renders "unwavering hearts." The Greek prothesis means "purpose" or "resolve." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "resolute heart" captures both the intentionality (prothesis) and the inward seat of commitment (kardia) in natural English.

12. **Verse 26 -- "Christians" (Christianous):** All three translators preserve this landmark designation. The verb chrēmatisai can mean "to be publicly called," "to bear a name," or even "to receive a name by divine direction." Whether the name was given by outsiders (perhaps as a label for "Christ-partisans") or adopted internally, Antioch is where the followers of Jesus first received a name distinguishing them from Judaism. SOPHIA preserves the passive construction: "the disciples were first called Christians."

13. **Verse 28 -- "the whole inhabited world" (holēn tēn oikoumenēn):** EMET renders "the whole inhabited-earth"; LOGOS renders "the whole inhabited world"; PNEUMA renders "the entire Roman world." The Greek oikoumenē technically means the inhabited world, which in first-century usage denoted the Roman Empire. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "the whole inhabited world" preserves the Greek term's scope without importing "Roman" (which is an interpretive addition, however accurate). The historical note "which came to pass under Claudius" provides sufficient context.

14. **Verse 29 -- "relief" (eis diakonian):** EMET renders "for ministry"; LOGOS renders "relief"; PNEUMA renders "relief." The Greek diakonia means "service" or "ministry," and here specifically refers to material aid. SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1): "relief" captures the specific sense of charitable assistance that the context demands.

15. **Verse 30 -- "elders" (presbyterous):** All three translators render presbyterous as "elders." This is the first mention of elders in the Jerusalem church in Acts, marking a significant development in ecclesial structure. SOPHIA concurs.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Word order / emphasis | "also the Gentiles received" | "the Gentiles also had received" | "the Gentiles too had received" | "the Gentiles also had received" -- follows LOGOS; natural English emphasis with "also" modifying the subject |
| 2 | "disputing" vs. "contended" vs. "challenged" | "were disputing with him" | "contended with him" | "challenged him" | "contended with him" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the diakrino root and formal register |
| 3 | "men having uncircumcision" vs. "uncircumcised men" | "men having uncircumcision" | "uncircumcised men" | "uncircumcised men" | "uncircumcised men" -- 2-to-1; English adjective captures the periphrasis naturally |
| 4 | Opening conjunction and phrasing | "And Peter having begun was explaining" | "But Peter began and explained" | "Peter began and laid the whole thing out" | "But Peter began and explained" -- follows LOGOS; "but" (de) marks the contrast with the accusation; PNEUMA too colloquial |
| 5 | "ecstasy" vs. "trance" | "in an ecstasy" | "in a trance" | "fell into a trance" | "in a trance" -- consistent with SOPHIA Acts 10:10 decision; "trance" is standard English for ekstasis |
| 5 | "linen-sheet" vs. "sheet" | "a great linen-sheet" | "a great sheet" | "a great sheet" | "a great sheet" -- 2-to-1; othonē refers to linen cloth but "sheet" suffices in English |
| 6 | Wild beasts (kai ta thēria) | Included | Included | Included | Included -- all three agree; Sinaiticus reads the four-category list; notable expansion from Acts 10:12 |
| 6 | "creeping-things" vs. "reptiles" | "creeping-things" | "creeping things" | "reptiles" | "creeping things" -- 2-to-1; herpeta means "things that creep"; "reptiles" is too taxonomically specific |
| 8 | "By no means" vs. "Absolutely not" | "By no means, Lord" | "By no means, Lord" | "Absolutely not, Lord!" | "By no means, Lord" -- 2-to-1; mēdamōs is a strong negative; both renderings work but "by no means" is more formal |
| 8 | "common or unclean" vs. "impure or unclean" | "common or unclean" | "common or unclean" | "impure or unclean" | "common or unclean" -- 2-to-1; consistent with SOPHIA Acts 10 decisions; preserves koinon/akatharton distinction |
| 9 | "make common" vs. "call common" vs. "call impure" | "you shall not make common" | "you must not call common" | "you must not call impure" | "you must not call common" -- follows LOGOS; consistent with Acts 10:15 decision |
| 11 | "in which I was" vs. "in which we were" | "in which I was" (ēmēn) | "in which we were" | "where I was staying" | "in which I was" -- follows EMET; Sinaiticus reads HMHN (first person singular); Peter speaks of his own location |
| 12 | "doubting nothing" vs. "making no distinction" vs. "without hesitation" | "doubting nothing" | "making no distinction" | "without hesitation" | "making no distinction" -- follows LOGOS; theological precision: the Spirit abolishes the Jew/Gentile barrier; echoes diakrino root from v. 2 |
| 13 | "send-for" vs. "summon" vs. "bring back" | "send-for" | "summon" | "bring back" | "summon" -- follows LOGOS; metapempsai means "to send for / summon"; PNEUMA's "bring back" is too loose |
| 17 | "equal gift" vs. "same gift" | "the equal gift" | "the same gift" | "the same gift" | "the same gift" -- 2-to-1; isēn communicates identity of the gift; "same" is natural English |
| 17 | "who was I to hinder God" phrasing | "who was I [to be] able to hinder God?" | "who was I to be able to hinder God?" | "who was I to stand in God's way?" | "who was I to be able to hinder God?" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; preserves dynatos kōlysai; PNEUMA's idiom loses the specific vocabulary |
| 18 | "repentance unto life" vs. dynamic rendering | "repentance unto life" | "repentance unto life" | "the chance to turn to him and live" | "repentance unto life" -- 2-to-1; preserves the precise theological formula metanoia eis zōēn |
| 19 | "tribulation" vs. "persecution" | "tribulation having happened over Stephen" | "tribulation that arose over Stephen" | "persecution that broke out over Stephen" | "tribulation that arose over Stephen" -- follows LOGOS; thlipsis means affliction/tribulation, not specifically persecution (diōgmos) |
| 20 | **Hellenistas vs. Hellenas (critical variant)** | "Greeks" (notes Sinaiticus: Hellenistas) | "Greeks" (notes variant: Hellenas) | "Greeks" (notes Sinaiticus: Hellenistas) | **"Hellenists"** -- SOPHIA preserves the Sinaiticus reading ELLHNISTAS; see Council Notes for full discussion; EMET and PNEUMA correctly identify the manuscript reading; LOGOS follows the variant |
| 22 | "word was heard in the ears" vs. "report came to the ears" | "the word was heard in the ears" | "the report concerning them came to the ears" | "News of this reached the ears" | "the report concerning them came to the ears" -- follows LOGOS; logos here means "report/account" rather than "the Word" |
| 23 | "purpose of heart" vs. "resolute heart" vs. "unwavering hearts" | "with purpose of heart" | "with resolute heart" | "with unwavering hearts" | "with resolute heart" -- follows LOGOS; captures prothesis tēs kardias naturally |
| 24 | "considerable crowd" vs. "large crowd" | "a considerable crowd" | "a considerable crowd" | "a large crowd" | "a considerable crowd" -- 2-to-1; ochlos hikanos means a substantial/considerable crowd |
| 25 | "went out" vs. "departed" vs. "went" | "he went out to Tarsus" | "he departed to Tarsus" | "Barnabas went to Tarsus" | "he departed to Tarsus" -- follows LOGOS; exēlthen can mean "went out" or "departed"; "departed" captures the deliberate journey |
| 26 | "were called" (chrēmatisai) | "were called Christians" | "were first called Christians" | "were first called Christians" | "were first called Christians" -- all three agree on the substance; chrēmatisai carries connotations of public designation |
| 28 | "inhabited-earth" vs. "inhabited world" vs. "Roman world" | "the whole inhabited-earth" | "the whole inhabited world" | "the entire Roman world" | "the whole inhabited world" -- follows LOGOS; oikoumenē = inhabited world; PNEUMA's "Roman world" imports an interpretive gloss |
| 28 | "was signifying" vs. "indicated" vs. "predicted" | "was signifying through the Spirit" | "indicated through the Spirit" | "predicted through the Spirit" | "indicated through the Spirit" -- follows LOGOS; esēmainen means "signified/indicated"; PNEUMA's "predicted" is too narrow |
| 29 | "for ministry" vs. "relief" | "for ministry" | "relief" | "relief" | "relief" -- 2-to-1; diakonia here specifically means material aid/assistance |
| 29 | "according as any was prospering" vs. "according as anyone had the means" | "according as any was prospering" | "according as anyone had the means" | "each according to their ability" | "according as anyone had the means" -- follows LOGOS; euporeito means "to have means/resources"; PNEUMA's paraphrase is clear but loose |
| 30 | "having sent" vs. "sending it" vs. "sending their gift" | "having sent to the elders" | "sending it to the elders" | "sending their gift to the elders" | "sending it to the elders" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "gift" which is not in the Greek |

-- SOPHIA
